lesson_title: Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text: To dynamically allocate a single integer and store its address: {{c1::int* ptr{ new int };}}
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text:
To deallocate dynamically allocated memory pointed to by ptr: {{c1::delete ptr;}}
Then set ptr to: {{c2::ptr = nullptr;}}
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.front: What are the three basic types of memory allocation in C++?
cards.back:
• Static memory allocation (for static and global variables)
  • Automatic memory allocation (for function parameters and local variables)
  • Dynamic memory allocation (using new/delete)
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.front: Where does dynamically allocated memory come from, and how does it compare to the stack?
cards.back: Dynamic memory comes from the heap, which is a much larger pool of memory managed by the operating system (can be gigabytes). The stack is typically limited (e.g., 1MB in Visual Studio by default).
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text:
Two ways to initialize a dynamically allocated int:
Direct initialization: {{c1::int* ptr1{ new int(5) };}}
Uniform initialization: {{c2::int* ptr2{ new int{6} };}}
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.back: A pointer that is pointing to deallocated memory
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: After delete, ptr becomes a dangling pointer. Dereferencing it causes undefined behavior.
cards.explanation: Once memory is deleted, the pointer still contains the old address but that memory has been returned to the OS. Accessing it is undefined behavior.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.front: What does the delete operator actually do to memory?
cards.back: It returns the memory being pointed to back to the operating system. It does NOT delete the pointer variable itself - the pointer variable still exists with the same scope.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.explanation: Set deleted pointers to nullptr unless they are going out of scope immediately afterward. This prevents accidentally using a dangling pointer.
examples: ptr = nullptr;  // now ptr is clearly not pointing to valid memory
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.back: When a program loses the address of dynamically allocated memory before deallocating it, making that memory impossible to delete
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.question: Why does this function cause a memory leak?
cards.answer: When the function ends, ptr goes out of scope and is destroyed, but the dynamically allocated memory is not deleted. The program loses the address and can never delete it.
cards.explanation: The pointer variable follows normal scoping rules and is destroyed, but the heap memory persists until explicitly deleted.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text:
To have new return nullptr instead of throwing an exception when allocation fails: {{c1::int* value{ new (std::nothrow) int{} };}}
Then check: {{c2::if (!value) { /* handle error */ }}}
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.front: What happens when you delete a null pointer?
cards.back: Nothing happens - it's safe and does nothing. There is no need to check if a pointer is null before deleting it.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.question: Why does this cause a memory leak?
cards.code:
int* ptr{ new int{} };
ptr = new int{};
cards.answer: The address from the second allocation overwrites the address of the first allocation. The first allocated memory can never be deleted - it's a memory leak.
cards.explanation: You must delete the first allocation before allocating again, or use a different pointer variable.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.front: Why is accessing heap-allocated objects generally slower than stack-allocated objects?
cards.back:
Stack objects: compiler knows the address directly, goes there in one step.
Heap objects: accessed via pointer, requires two steps: (1) get address from pointer, (2) access value at that address.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.concept: When to use dynamic memory allocation
cards.explanation:
Use dynamic allocation when:
  • The size of memory needed is not known at compile time (e.g., user input)
  • You need more memory than the stack can provide (stack is limited, often ~1MB)
  • You need memory that persists beyond the current scope
  • Array size needs to be determined at runtime
examples: char name[25];          // bad: fixed size, wasted memory
char* name{ new char[length] };  // good: size from user input
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text: The three types of memory allocation: {{c1::static}} (global/static vars), {{c2::automatic}} (local vars/params), {{c3::dynamic}} (heap allocation)
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text: Dynamic memory is allocated from the {{c1::heap}}, which can be {{c2::gigabytes}} in size, much larger than the {{c3::stack}} (typically ~1MB).
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete

cards.text: Best practice: {{c1::Set deleted pointers to nullptr}} unless they are going out of scope immediately afterward.
cards.source: 19.1 - Dynamic memory allocation with new and delete
